narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 31, you've shown determination in pursuing law school, including your background from a middle-income family and your plan to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model, which predicts whether applicants like you are likely to pass the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.2% chance that you would fail it—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes those with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most to the model. Your undergraduate GPA was the single biggest positive factor, pushing strongly against a failure prediction because it's above the typical 3.21 average, signaling solid academic preparation from your undergrad days. On the flip side, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of 5.53—was the most influential negative factor, pulling the prediction toward failure by highlighting relatively weaker performance in that later stage compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the average of 36.70, also worked against you, though less powerfully than your Year 3 decile, nudging the odds slightly toward a fail. Overall, while your GPA gave you a real edge over many applicants, the combination of your lower Year 3 decile and LSAT score outweighed it, placing you below the threshold for predicted bar passers that the school favors. This mirrors patterns in the data where students with consistently higher deciles and LSATs tend to succeed on the bar first time around. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile standing. Building a clearer narrative around your upward trajectory from Year 3 could also help in reapplying, perhaps with letters emphasizing your resilience as a full-time student from a middle-income background. Many applicants in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your strong GPA is already a foundation to build on. With targeted improvements, you can shift that 45.2% failure probability in your favor for the next cycle.
